summary: nock-helper postinstall harvests npm tokens, env secrets, git credentials, and browser crypto wallets; exfiltrates to hardcoded C2
details: package.json declares "postinstall": "node postinstall.js", which runs on every `npm install`. postinstall.js reads ~/.npmrc (extracting _authToken and npm_ tokens), ~/.env (categorising TOKEN/API_KEY/DB_URL/PAYMENT/CLOUD/EMAIL/WEBHOOK entries and EVM private keys), ~/.git-credentials, and scans Chrome/Brave/Edge/Chromium/Firefox user profiles against 71 hardcoded crypto wallet extension IDs (MetaMask, Phantom, Coinbase, Trust, Ledger, Trezor, Solflare, etc.) to pull LevelDB contents. It also searches the home directory for seed-phrase and private-key shaped files. Harvested data plus os.hostname() and os.userInfo().username are JSON-encoded and POSTed to the hardcoded bare-IP C2 http://149.28.127.35:8888 over plain HTTP with no authentication or pinning (C2 constant at postinstall.js:7, `const C2=process.env.C2_URL||'http://149.28.127.35:8888'`). The file's own header comment describes it as 'Token harvester + Crypto wallet scanner / Runs on npm install. Silent. Zero trace.' index.js is a dummy stub that self-identifies as 'lodash-js' with the comment 'The real payload is in postinstall.js'. The package name also resembles the popular HTTP-mocking library `nock`, forming a typosquat lure.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
